Document [3] (Title: Lattice Corneal Dystrophy -- Treatment / Management) Gene therapies are also under development and may soon be the mainstay of treatment for corneal dystrophies. Significant progress has been made in identifying specific mutations associated with LCD. Because of this progress, therapies targeted at these specific mutations are becoming more feasible. Chaperone nanobodies, siRNA, CRISPER, and antisense oligonucleotides have all been suggested as possible treatments for LCD. So far, siRNA and nanobodies show promise for type I and type II LCD, respectively. Antisense oligonucleotides and CRISPR show promise in investigations for treatment of other corneal disorders, but have not yet been investigated for use in LCD. Document [16] (Title: Cat Eye Syndrome (Schmid-Fraccaro Syndrome) -- Introduction) From a research perspective, CES provides valuable insights into gene dosage effects in contiguous-gene syndromes. The 22q11 region implicated in CES overlaps with that of DiGeorge syndrome (22q11.2 deletion), yet their phenotypes differ, reflecting divergent consequences of haploinsufficiency versus gene overexpression. Current studies focus on elucidating the roles of candidate genes such as CECR1 (which encodes adenosine deaminase 2) and CECR2 (involved in chromatin remodeling) in contributing to CES manifestations. Animal models with targeted duplications of the 22q11 region are under development to investigate relevant developmental pathways. Additionally, next-generation sequencing techniques show promise in detecting cryptic rearrangements and refining genotype–phenotype correlations, ultimately improving prognostic accuracy and identifying potential therapeutic targets. Document [21] (Title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 698) The choroidea (choroid) is repeatedly identified as the primary anatomic site for a broad spectrum of ocular pathologies. Inflammatory disorders localized to the choroid include disseminated choroiditis, focal choroiditis, generalized disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis, peripheral disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis, metastatic disseminated retinitis‑retinochoroiditis, as well as combined entities such as التهاب المشيمية (choroiditis), التهاب المشيمية والشبكية (choroiditis‑retinitis), and the unspecified focal variant التهاب المشيمية والشبكية البؤري، غير محدد. Post‑inflammatory and post‑traumatic sequelae—post‑inflammatory chorioretinal scar, post‑inflammatory choroid scar, and post‑traumatic chorioretinal scar—are also situated in the choroidal tissue. Degenerative and structural changes affecting the choroidea comprise تنكس حصوي (calcific degeneration), تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة (choroidal degeneration), and choroidal sclerosis around the optic disc in both partial (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي) and total (حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي) forms. Vascular complications recorded as choroidal findings include نزيف مشيمي باثق (persistent choroidal hemorrhage), hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي), and non‑hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين). The choroidea is additionally noted to have a relationship with بُقَعُ روث, although the clinical significance of this association is not detailed. Moreover, the choroidea is described in Arabic as “حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي,” indicating that pathological observations may be localized to a specific genetic chorionic region. Recognizing the choroid as the common locus for these inflammatory, infectious, traumatic, degenerative, vascular, and post‑injury scarring processes—and for its genetic and ancillary associations—is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted imaging, and appropriate therapeutic planning in ophthalmic practice.
